comments.title: Are you raising your geomanctic voice at me??
comments.content:
Some months ago we were speculating on the importance of geomantic charts that repeat themselves - i.e. the daughters are the same as the mothers, and in the same order. But I've had something even stranger happen this month and wonder if you might give me your .02 on the matter.

On May 10th - Mother's Day, the day I smashed my Android to pieces with a hammer - I cast my usual house chart for the day and got a repeating pattern. Then I got another one the following day. Saturday, May 23rd, I got another one. Then another one the following morning. (I may have had 4 total in the previous 8 months I've been studying geomancy.) So that makes twice this month I've had not only repeating charts, but repeating charts on back-to-back days. (I would assume the pattern has run its course - double charts, double days, twice this month - but who knows.)

The turning point in my life that occurred on Mother's Day, and it's been a mighty fine one, led me to believe that perhaps the repeating chart is how the geomantic oracle raises its voice - either in warning or salute. The last notable repeating chart I had was last October, when I asked if I should give up smoking weed. I got the repeating pattern and an occupation saying basically "hell yes, you should quit smoking weed." I didn't, and then descended into still-ongoing months of prostate pain and discomfort, always inflamed by smoking the herb. (I finally gave it up at the astrological new year and am slowly improving, btw.)

But the pattern was interesting enough to me that I cast a follow-up chart this morning (Sunday) asking, "Are these repeating charts on repeating days trying to tell me something important?" Here is the result:

1: Puella
2: Cauda
3: Puella
4: Carcer
5: Via
6: Rubeus
7: Cauda
8: Puella
9: Acquisitio
10: Albus
11: Puella
12: Acquisitio

I worked under the assumption that this is a 9th house matter (divination), but the 3rd house also seems relevant. So that yields Acquisitio-Puella conjunctions in 8-9 and 12-1, a mutation to 11-12, a sextile between 9-11, an opposition between 3-9, and a square between 3-12.

Obviously the chart has plenty to say, and I have some ideas (as an aside, Rubeus tends to show up in the 6th when the oracle wants to address my prostate issues), but I could really use some help diagnosing this one. If you would, please, sir.

And thank you so much.
Grover

There's a note in my journal last night that says, "But why the repeating pattern? Why raise one's voice over such a mundane day?"

I guess my next step is to go back and see if I can tease out any relationships between the 4 charts - figures, houses, etc. I did go back to my last prostate diagnostic chart to fine-tune my analysis. Once one gets clued into to the fact that divination actually communicates something, it becomes easy to look around the world about you for clues for an answer pretty much anywhere— it’s all just a matter of who (or what) you want to ask. I still remember my first time playing with this concept— I had a pot of assorted herbal teas, and I was suddenly taken with the notion that if those oracle cards over there worked so well, couldn’t I just I reach in and get a message from whatever tea I picked? comments.content:
Hello JMG,

1. Are there any noteworthy magical workings you’ve done that you could share with us? It doesn’t have to be anything personally significant to you. A moderately complex working that you performed that you think worked out well and could be a learning experience for others would be sufficient. Could you give, for example, a description of the ritual, the materials used, what your intentions were, at what stage of development you were when you undertook it, how it turned out, and so on? I think it would beneficial for novices to see how a “real” magician approaches the art.

2. Since my work with the Tarot has been ramping up lately, I’ve come up with the following attribution scheme for the minor arcana: All Cups to Netzach, all Swords to Hod, all Wands to Yesod, all Pentacles to Malkuth. My motivation for doing this is that I wasn't satisfied with the traditional attribution of the minor arcana to the Sephiroth (all twos to Chokmah, all threes to Binah, etc.) I’m thinking that this maps fairly well between the four suits, four classical elements, four cognitive functions (feeling, thinking, intuition, sensing), and four Sephiroth of the personality in incarnation. The downside is that it ignores the higher self (Tiphareth, Geburah, Chesed) and spiritual self (Binah, Chokmah, Kether). Do you feel like this attribution has any potential?

3. Do entities on the higher planes operate under a Darwinian paradigm? Is there natural or sexual selection and competition among angels, gods, and other transcendental beings, or is that only something we have to deal with at the monkey level and below?

4. Does someone have to be a higher achiever to move up to the next level of incarnation in the afterlife? Is it all about having a highly developed inner life, or does the inner life need to bear fruit in practical achievements or it to make a difference?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) That would be a subject for a full-length post on its own, and I'd have to review the ritual text and then go back through my practice journals to check on how it went and what the results were. I'll consider it, but it isn't something I can answer off the cuff.

2) Only you can decide that. I'd certainly encourage you to explore that attribution, though.

3) Nothing I've read or experienced suggests that this is the case.

4) There are no fixed rules. If you've already had lives of high outward achievement, for example, a life purely devoted to inner growth may be what you need this time around.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I'm a mid-20s young man from an absurdly privileged social class (my parents have a six figure tax bill); and I'm miserable. I've tried changing my life, but every single time run into a mix of internal and external push back: I've realized I've internalized most of the bigotries and idiocies of my social background, and am floundering about, struggling with even the most basic changes.

One thing which causes me problems in particular is that I'm terrified of being "poor", and "poor" to my mind is what a lot of people would consider well off (and not just in poor countries!)

It's finally occurred to me that the best approach is to embrace "poverty", and so I'm looking for an apartment, and plan on moving out in August, and I will be living with only the resources I have at hand: in particular this mean, no regular support from my parents anymore (I will still accept birthday and Christmas money; it would be too much of a hassle to refuse, and I'll put it aside in savings). This will likely mean a grocery budget of around 50 dollars a week, which is a far cry from where my family is at, at around 200 per person per week; and a range of other financial restrictions which will take a good deal of getting used to.

I'm journaling to see if I can reach the root cause, and working through LRM and will take more potent steps to address me issues later, but for now, I am slowly working at fixing this issue, and hope embracing it will help speed the process.

1) The inspiration behind taking this step is your book Green Wizardry. Particularly the part where you talk about people who talk about how horrible middle class lifestyles are and don't bother to change. It felt like you were talking to me directly and giving me the hard truth and calling me personally out for it: thank you for those harsh but necessary words.

Did you write that part with the idea of reaching people like me?

2) Is it best to look for an apartment on Fridays, preferably during an hour of Venus, or am I on the wrong track?

3) From a mundane perspective, do you have advice on being poor in style? I figure if I can make it pleasant, it'll help with getting over the fear of it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
First of all, I'm delighted to hear this. The terror of being poor is an iron chain that keeps many people from having a life. Once you know you can get by on very little, you've got an enormous amount of freedom, and then you can start deciding what you want to do with your life.

1) You're welcome. Yes, that was why I wrote that.

2) Start your apartment hunt during an hour of Venus on Fridays. Beginnings are crucial. After that, keep at it -- the energy will stay with you.

3) Yes, I do -- I had the very great advantage of spending a decade or so being very poor indeed, and got fairly good at it. The first thing to remember is that you're not alone, and there are a vast number of resources available to help you out. I don't mean handouts! Try shopping at thrift stores and cheap groceries in lower middle class neighborhoods; get books from the public library instead of buying them; look into free or low-cost options for your other needs and wants -- they're out there.

The second is that you need much less than you probably think you do. You can thrive very well on a diet of cheap homecooked foods like rice and beans, steamed vegetables, and cheap cuts of meat such as chicken thighs. You probably have many more clothes than you need, and most entertainment is there to keep you from having a life. Winnow things down to the point that you know what you need and can tell the difference between "need" and "want," and you've got a resource most people in your class never discover.

Third, make lots of room for your own creative and spiritual activities. One of the great advantages to withdrawing from the status game is that you have time and space for such things. Practice magic if that appeals to you, take up drawing or painting or music or what have you -- you've got an entire life ahead of you and you're about to take the step that will throw open the doors and let you pursue that life. Relax, have fun, and enjoy some rice and beans!

replies.title: A View From the Lower Middle Class
replies.content:
I'm a former member of the upper-middle class. My parents were never as well off as yours but back in the 80s, they did pretty well before the bottom fell out. I'm 46 now and I am now in a much lower class and neighborhood than the ones I grew up in. To make a long story short, in my opinion, you have absolutely nothing to be afraid of.

I own a small business that does fine but because of the high costs of running it, makes very little profit. My husband was unemployed for a period of three years. I couldn't even afford our studio apartment. Consequently, we lived with relatives when we were both considered far too old for that sort of thing. Then we somehow managed to buy ourselves a small house in a neighborhood that is considered sketchy by my old upper middle class schoolmates and neighbors. Sometimes I have to make a $50 food budget last one month for two people: been there, done that. You'd be amazed at how good the food is if you are creative and see the budget as a challenge instead of a hindrance.

It isn't the worst thing. Attitude is key. Meanwhile, I see my childhood pals, still wealthy, bitterly griping about Trump's latest peccadillo while they refuse to address the roots of their class bigotry: fear of becoming poor. Much of their misery and depression can be blamed on their handicap when it comes to appreciating the comparative wealth of our time. When I eat strawberries in winter, I take a moment to consider Roman empresses were not able to do that. I have similar thoughts when eating chocolate, driving my car wherever I like, and playing one of my beautifully made yet affordable guitars. I don't see my chronically anxious childhood peers giving gratitude anything more than lip service, yet gratitude makes all the difference in the world.

replies.title: gratitude and getting used to less
replies.content:
If you are in reasonable health and you are ambulatory enough to walk over uneven ground, and you have a pair of what used to be called tennis shoes, or comfortable walking shoes, or boots, I have a suggestion for later on, when you have made your move and have been living small enough to miss some of the things you can't afford.

Go camping for four days to a week-- in a hike in campground without showers if you have no previous camping experience, or perhaps somewhere more remote and less developed if you tent camped before. Pick a time of the year when it's unlikely to rain on you and you won't need to carry a tent.

Basic camping equipment to keep you fed, hydrated and give you enough comfort to sleep would be a sleeping bag or bed roll, a small closed cell foam mattress or some other kind of sleeping pad to cushion the bumps and give you some thermal insulation from the cold ground, a lightweight saucepan, plate or bowl, drinking cup, spoon and fork or spork, a pocket knife or something similar, a small flashlight, biodegradable soap, food and drink that requires no refrigeration and can be eaten cold or prepared by pouring hot water over it, a canteen or water bottle, water for drinking and cooking and washing hands if there is no potable water at your campsite, maybe a dish pan that you can empty out in a bush far from the stream after you clean your dishes, map, compass if you might be going off trail, bandanna. Bandannas are multipurpose tools: hat, washcloth, hot pad, etc. If your knife has no can opener and you bring cans, you will need one. Maybe a small first aid kit.

ETA If campfire is not possible, something to heat water, such as Sterno cans and a stand for your saucepan or kettle, or a backpacker's stove, but those tend to be pricy. I understand that Army MRE's have their own heating source. Of course, in good weather hot food is not a necessity, but the ability to boil water or heat food in a saucepan or frying pan makes for happier diners.

Something to carry all this stuff on your back. If need be, you can roll your bedroll lengthwise maybe with your clothes inside, tie with string in several places, (don't use knots you can't untie and retie), put everything else into a small knapsack from the thrift store, tie the bedroll in an upside down U over the pack.

Matches if the place allows you to make a campfire and it is safe to do so. Do not make a campfire if you have not been taught in the Scouts or some other reliable place where to build one, how to build a very small fire, and how to put it out thoroughly before you go to bed. Never leave the fire or the hot/warm coals unattended. Do not try this without personal instruction.

Basic toiletries and whatever meds you need, insect repellent, sunscreen if you need it, hat, change of underwear and clothing. One or two field guides (stars, plants, birds) if you want.

If there is no outhouse at your campsite, then you need to bring a half roll of TP and a garden trowel or the like and learn ahead of time the appropriate location and depth to dig a cat hole for feces and toilet paper.

Pack out every single thing you pack in except your bodily wastes.

John Muir camped with a lot less gear than this, but he grew up poor and rugged, and he may have cut bough beds, which is not allowed today except perhaps deep in the Maine woods.

Make camp, eat what you brought with you, take walks, sleep (or try to sleep), under the night stars.

You might enjoy primitive camping a great deal. You might dislike it or be indifferent to it. Four days to a week will certainly help you appreciate hot showers and mattresses or whatever modern conveniences you have. OTOH, if you live in a rat, cockroach and bedbug infested room with bad plumbing down the hall, you'll know that you can get a break from it once in awhile.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Congratulations!

There are SO MANY resources out there on how to make it work. You are in good company!

We're not stylish, but we're comfortably poor. Here's what has been most helpful to us:

--Thrift stores are great. But you should also check out consignment stores! Most of them focus on womens' clothing, but there is often a small section of men's clothing and shoes, and it'll tend to be in better condition, and better quality, than what you find in most thrift stores-- and often just as cheap. It takes some practice, but if you can learn to spot good quality clothing that still has a lot of wear left in it, you may never want to buy a new shirt or jeans again. Thrift and consignment are also great for furniture, dishes, linens (bleach them), flowerpots, replacement computer keyboards, and even office supplies (I wear out a steady stream of 3-ring binders, and I get them for a quarter each at the local Habitat for Humanity ReStore).

--Find out where the poor folks shop in your area. Check out all the ethnic grocery shops-- they often have better, cheaper produce than anyone else.

--Cook for yourself, and cook a lot! It takes about the same amount of time and energy to roast two chicken thighs as it does to roast a dozen. If you do a dozen, now you have dinner, tomorrow's lunch, and three more meals in the freezer to warm up quick or eat cold picnic-style. The very cheapest meal you purchase "out" is orders of magnitude more expensive than something equivalent cooked at home, and having a few quick already-cooked things tucked away in the freezer heads off a lot of takeout temptation. Wherever you go, it's worth packing a lunch and bringing your own drinking vessel to fill with water. Cultivate a taste for water, if you haven't already. It's good for you ;)

--Someone already mentioned it, but: read Your Money or Your Life! Their investment advice is completely outdated and hasn't been workable since the 90s, but everything else there is transformative. If your library has any of the Tightwad Gazette books, they're worth pawing through as well. She is more oriented toward budget living for a family, but the same principles apply, and she radically changed the way I approach grocery shopping. I don't use her detailed system any more, but it was an extremely useful intermediary step for me. They also hammer home the importance of buying quality vs. buying cheap.

--Give gifts! Cookies, fresh-baked bread, tomato plants or herbs in pots (I grow them from seed)... these make great gifts, and they're a great way to get to know your neighbors. And you need to get to know your neighbors: they are the best security system you can have. When you're rich, money is your safety net. When you're poor, it's the people you know and with whom you exchange gifts, favors, and information.

Best of luck on your transition!

replies.title: Re: Uncooperative Geomancy
replies.content:
Boulderchum,

If you go back a few months in the Magic Monday archive, you might find an amusingly embarrassing question from me regarding my own overwrought reading of a geomancy chart, in which I feared an everyday set of figures predicted tragic events. So, for what comes next, please bear in mind that, for one, I am not an expert with years under my belt; but likewise, I've been at a similar place to where you are now very recently ("Oh no! My chart perfected, but the book says perfection by negative figures means I won't be happy about it!") and I hope my perspective might also be of some help.

1. You asked if you should take Bardon's course, and got a clear "No".

2. You asked "Is one of JMG's paths right for me?" The answer: Yes, by the very strongest form of perfection, Occupation.

3. You asked "Should I take JMG's ~~ course?", one by one, and the CGD came up with perfection by Conjunction (2-9) AND Conjunction (1-10) AND Mutation (10-11) AND Mutation (11-12), with positive sextile aspect (1-11). Even accounting for the single measly little square (9-12), you've literally got Perfection in five (!!!!!) different ways there. Five!

4. You've gotten answers as clear as Geomancy is capable of here, but you're apparently afraid to accept them, so then you ask, "What course should I commit to?" and unsurprisingly get Rubeus in the first house. (You already got a positive answer, but you're refusing to accept it for some reason-- this would surely be enough to spark a Rubeus, I should think.)

It seems that you are not satisfied with these crystal clear and unequivocal readings which are giving you unambiguous answers to your questions, because of the particular figures involved. Do you require everything to perfect with occupation by Fortuna Major in order to go forward on a path? Of course not, right? Let's look at the figures you got.

For the first, you asked, "Is one of JMG's paths right for me?"Now, first of all, you didn't ask "Is one of JMG's paths going to be easy fun for me every step of the way?"-- you just asked if it is "right" for you, whatever that means to you and the geomantic intelligences. I'll just point out that that Tristitia is very positive for matters involving stability and patience, as well as for matters involving working with the earth (hello Druids!), where permanence is wanted, and keeping secrets (aka occultism). While Tristitia points to some hard work and probably some difficult experiences, I should think that's all par for the course if you're seriously interested in pursuing a path which brings you through a journey of meaningful personal change.

For the second CGD question, I fail to even see what's scary about these. Populus in the first house is neither positive nor negative (and is unsurprising in this case, as you are somewhat passively asking Geomancy to guide your decision here), and as it tends to take on the qualities of the houses surrounding it (Caput Draconis, definitely positive in this case), then all is good. Is it those three passionate Rubeus in houses 6, 7, and 8, none of which mark the question being asked? There *might* be a warning packed in there somewhere, e.g. to be careful of your health if you follow this path (6), or to take care to keep your oaths (and use a legal copy of the book) (7), or to carefully think through any workings before embarking on them (8). But they are purely secondary to your main question. As for that-- Caput Draconis, the symbol of fertile beginnings, in the 9th house! It doesn't get much better than that.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: This very likely displays a complete misunderstanding of karma, but you've mentioned rejecting collective responsibility, and yet have mentioned believing in collective karma. Would you mind explaining the differences?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The concept of collective responsibility, as invented by antisemites during the Middle Ages and more recently deployed by social justice activists, amounts to this: "Your ancestors did something really awful and therefore we get to treat you as vilely as we can, while still claiming to be the good guys." It's a covert concept of collective irresponsibility -- people who use it are trying to avoid responsibility for their own hateful acts.

The concept of collective karma, by contrast, simply means that what a community or a society does shapes the character of that community or society, and tends to bring experiences that respond to that character. If a community spends its time hating and persecuting, it's going to become more hateful and intolerant, and other communities will respond accordingly -- as will natural phenomena such as climate and disease. If those other communities end up hating and persecuting their hateful and intolerant neighbors, though, they don't get a free pass. The challenge facing those other communities is learning the lesson that Krishna taught Arjuna in the Bhagavad-Gita, how to fight evil without becoming evil.

replies.title: Fighting Evil
replies.content:
Hi JMG,

How does a person (or community) fight evil without becoming evil?

Your pithy explanation would be appreciated.

Aquamarine Crunchy Platypus (OtterGirl)

replies.title: Re: Fighting Evil
replies.content: Center your strategy and tactics on the good you want to defend or achieve, not the evil you want to get rid of. Use the least destructive approach that will get the job done, and always be open to the possibility that the other side may have valid reasons too -- a possibility that very often opens the door to a negotiated settlement. If you have to use force, be as precise as a surgeon -- take out what you have to take out, avoiding collateral damage as much as you can. Always ask yourself what's going to happen after the fight is over.

replies.title: Re: Greek pantheon for SOP?
replies.content:
The Greek pantheon's pretty flexible, and iirc the people who are working with it have all made their own choices. I'd probably do this:

East: Apollo
West: Dionysos
South: Athena
North: Aphrodite
Below: Hera
Above: Zeus

Of course you can swap these out for others if you like, though I recommend maintaining the gender balance.

As for the opening and closing, if you want to establish a good relationship with Hecate, she's three-faced and three-formed, so why not invoke her by three of her titles?

comments.title: Responses to last week
comments.content:
If I may, responses to a couple of posts from last week.

1) https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/88525.html?thread=8763853#cmt8763853
"Assuming one has done all the psychological work, and progressed with their life, is there a way to banish the symptoms (physical, dreams, emotional triggers etc.) of a traumatic childhood experience?"

Have a look into EMDR. It can seem, well, magical when it works. It is exactly these sorts of things, the physical and emotional residue, that it is good for getting rid of. It is done with a therapist but once you have got the hang of it, and depending how you respond to it, there is kit you can buy to continue it at home. This should be done carefully and with self awareness, similarly to how we are advised here to approach the occult.

2) https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/88525.html?thread=8717773#cmt8717773
Reply to the last post by the person concerned about neglecting minor household duties and having a sulky inner teenager:

As JMG said, this sounds like the way authoritarian parents might treat a child. That is not the only way to do things, and not a good one.

Why do you need to punish yourself further? The consequences or worrying about them are punishments in themselves.
What are the actual consequences of these things and do they matter to you?
Like butter going off more quickly, worrying that burglars might come in and that your insurance would be invalidated.
You seem to be demanding complete perfection of yourself, when it isn't necessary in most of these things to be a competent adult.

Why not allow yourself a few days a month to miss some of these tasks, especially the less consequential of them? This may help you feel more relaxed and the house is unlikely to go to rack and ruin because of it.
It sounds like you are trying to create too many habits at once.

Also, why see the reincarnation cycle this way? "kindergarten just keeps going on and on. The prospect of enduring thousands upon thousands of painful, ugly lives in order to micro-inch towards a ‘graduate’ level of being that merely entails still more responsibility and danger and hard work"
You can also look at it as an exciting opportunity to be immersed in many different experiences, and that each one will give you skills that will make dealing with these issues easier. You do have to do some work, yes, but that's not all there is to it.

replies.title: Re: Responses to last week
replies.content:
Same poster again. I'd been thinking of adding to this but wanted to wait until it was posted by JMG so I could reply to it.

How interesting that somebody else had also posted about EMDR a little earlier today, and that also didn't show up until now.

Just after I wrote the above I wondered why I hadn't said anything about EMDR in this community before as it is such a good fit, and I felt that it was important to encourage more people here to look into it. The set of posts on experimenting with cell salts, and seeing people trying it out, felt like the prompt to post about it further.

Firstly, EMDR in its basic form as first discovered via simple eye movements - though it must surely have been a rediscovery - is both simple to do, and a useful coping tool to have and to pass on in a collapse. Even the more hi-tec bits of kit for multi-sensory use could be made at home by a person with electronics skills, and appropriate technology versions could be made. Possibly the old cartoon meme of the watch on a chain being swung by a hypnotist was a version of it.

Secondly, it is a similar headspace to magic, meditation and lucid dreaming, though things shift much more quickly in it - assuming you are among the majority of people it works for.

It is also fairly accepted by the mainstream although some still find it too "woo", a space which seems like it would sit well here with those who require some evidence but are generally open minded to things that others have had success with.

It can potentially be done oneself, and learned from books which establish protocols and routines for it. Roaming around in that headspace with no structure is likely not a good idea for anybody new to it or who has many past traumatic experiences they haven't thoroughly processed.
Though those with serious problems, especially traumas they have tried to suppress and find it difficult to think about (avoidant), or who have experience dissociation or other bad freakouts, shouldn't start out on their own. Whereas if you can't stop thinking about the thing anyway (preoccupied), it is usually closer to the surface.

replies.title: Re: Responses to last week
replies.content:
First, thanks for your interest. I’ll try to address your concerns briefly. Second, I think JMG’s intuitive/experiential answer was spot on that emotional fog was fostering chronic absent mindedness. Third, I fear you may be giving the matter more importance than it deserves.

Based on the intelligence of your questions, I deduce that you have experience helping people release unwanted behavior and possess a large warm heart. Please do not be overly concerned about the awkward, cranky Rube Goldberg contraptions I use to motivate myself to do stuff I heartily dislike doing. They ain’t elegant but they usually work all right.

I do not see any harm in being authoritarian, per se. Sometimes putting my foot down is the only way to get the dern thing done. Especially when one is self-indulgent in other ways! Perfection is not a dirty word. It just means doing a job well. Soldiers do not get to say “I forgot” if they fall asleep on guard duty. Steve Martin does not get to say “I forgot” to the IRS for failing to file his taxes. Fog or no fog, keeping my word is important to me.

As for the low-down feelings about the reincarnation cycle, well: it’s my experience that trying to deny the way I feel is futile and aggravating. I find it better just to go ahead and feel what I feel. One can shove feelings aside by a heroic act of will, apply conscious effort to reframe the context of experience, etc. But why work so hard when I can just let them be? Feelings come and go. Sometimes they stink. (shrugs) So what? They’re not alligators. They won’t kill ya. They have their own place in the ecosystem too. They also serve who only rot into compost.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I have been meditating for the past couple of weeks on the Wheel of the Year, the Stations, and the Wheel of Life, and trying to connect them to some of the philosophy in the Dolmen Arch, and some of the correspondences of the Stations in the Druidry Handbook. All this is to prepare for enchanting the stones with the nwyfre of the 8 stations for the grove ceremony.

These thoughts came about because I noticed that the symbols for Sammhuinn and for Alban Heruin are respectively a cauldron and the three rays. The Wheel forms a loop, a binary between two stations, in which the two currents are mediated. After reading the philosophy in the Dolmen Arch, I realized that the Wheel of Life could symbolize the journey between Annwn (Sammhuinn - the cauldron) to Awen (Alban Heruin - the three rays) and back again. Awen is point of ultimate manifestation, Annwn the point of ultimate potential, the unmanifest, but the Wheel of the Year/Day/Month (or the Wheel for any process) is a cycle between the point where that process gets closest to Awen and the point of where it gets closest to Annwn.

But I noticed that the stations are not symmetrical, ie, Sammhuinn does not stand opposite to Alban Heruin, Alban Arthuan does. This threw me, until I understood that Sammhuinn is the slide from manifestation outwards until the point of least manifestation, so the 6 week period of Sammhuinn builds toward and ends in the unmanifest, the Winter Solstice, and the 6 week period of Alban Heruin starts at the point of highest manifestation, and fades away from it.

In the Druidry Handbook, the correspondences there for the places and energy centers of the body gave me another clue. Alban Arthuan corresponds to the heavens, and to the crown center in the body (hence its symbol), and possibly also to, in DA terms, the One Life of nwyfre. But the next station, Imbolc, corresponds to a cavern, to calas, and to the energy center in the feet. So there is an ascending motion through the Stations where the Stations correspond to higher energy centers in the body and places, and this doesn't end at Sammhuinn, but rather recommences at the point between Alban Arthuan and Imbolc. This makes the whole Wheel a loop, but if you 'octave shift' this Station upwards or downwards, the cycle begins at a higher or lower layer and the loop becomes a spiral.

This lead to other thoughts: the Wheel spins around a central point. What is that point? The Earth rotates on its axis every day, which is symbolized by the Wheel of the Year at the scale of the day. The central point is the center of the earth, which itself is orbiting the sun. I know, this is not physics, but it made me think that the central point of a wheel is also always in motion, and forms another cycle at a different scale. Thus, the loop of the turning of the Earth becomes a spiral through space, if that makes sense. This in turn led me to the as-yet-unresolved thought that perhaps a ternary often can resolve a binary by the fact that the third factor that resolves the binary is this cycle at a higher (or lower) scale.

So the Wheel of the Year and the Wheel of Life are arbitrary yet 'true' ways of dividing up the infinite 'Stations' of natural cycles, and describe how they interlock with other cycles. Each of the 8 Stations use the symbolism of seasons/times of day/etc to describe how those Stations behave and feel.

Yet I realized after reading this week's post on the other blog about alchemy that the observer factor is of great importance - I always thought that the mysteries presented in magical philosophy were entirely objective truths hidden in the text that needed to be puzzled out through meditation in a manner kind of like treasure hunting, but that's not the entire story. Your reminder that these are tools, not truths, and the idea of alchemy being a participatory science ('in which the observer and the observed both join in a shared process that transforms them both', to quote from your post) made me aware that, in the results of meditations as above, that observer factor which joins these thoughts together doesn't have to be 'right', it just has to be 'useful'.

With that disclaimer in mind, have I misunderstood anything in trying to tie together all these thoughts? Any hints as to next directions - is there anything in there that particularly bears further thought?

As always, thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: My job at this point is to sit back, say something generically encouraging, and let you keep going. This is exactly the sort of exploration that's appropriate for students who've mastered the basic symbolic structures and are trying to integrate them. Just remember that the process is more important that the result, and that all these insights -- useful as they are -- will be modified as you proceed further and deeper.

replies.title: Re: The Three Circles of Manifestation and the Ancestors
replies.content:
Many times man lives and dies
Between his two eternities
That of race and that of soul...

That's what William Butler Yeats wrote in his final poetic testament, "Under Ben Bulben." Each of us, while we live, are the intersection between two currents of life: a current of biological life descending from our ancestors, and a current of spiritual life descending from our previous incarnations. While you are in your current body, the ancestors of that body are relevant to you, and yes, ancestor reverence is an old and valuable custom. In your next life you will probably have different ancestors, though.

How does that work? The Japanese, who combine a robust Buddhist faith in reincarnation with an equally robust Shinto custom of ancestor reverence, have been trying to work out the details for centuries. All we know for sure is that both seem to be relevant.

comments.content:
Not so much a question as a comment in support. I also regard Watkins' "The Old Straight Track" as a major contribution to knowledge. (Of course, as is so often the case, Watkins himself was far more sensible than some of his followers and popularizers.)

We know from living Polynesian traditions of ocean voyages just how detailed and complex the means can be that pre-literate peoples have used to get from place to place. In the Polynesian case, ocean navigation was not only done in terms of stars and the sun and moon, but in terms of very real, obvious differences in the "look and feel"--and taste and smell--of ocean water from one place in the ocean to another.

If one can do this even on the fluid surface of the Pacific ocean, how much easier it could be to do the same sort of thing on the solid surface of the land in England and the rest of Eurasia.

On another note, a second part of this same body of prehistoric knowledge may recently have been uncovered by a man named Graham Robb, in his book variously titled "Ancient Paths" or "The Discovery of Middle Earth." It's about "lines of voice" across the landscape, places where echo patterns and so forth facilitate very long-distance hearing of shouted messages. (I haven't had time to read it yet, alas!)

Robb, like Watkins, seems to be dismissed out of hand by some professional prehistorians. Sometimes this can be a good sign.

replies.title: Re: Waning Piscean Influence
replies.content: The Piscean current is waning but, as my Christian readers have cogently pointed out, it's not dead yet. Several centuries will pass before it fades out entirely. Exactly what will happen to the various Piscean themes is a complex question that I don't think anyone alive today can answer. For now, though, if those themes are meaningful to you, work with them; you can explore other themes in a later incarnation.

comments.title: Asperger’s karma
comments.content:
I mentioned on the last open post on the main blog that I have just been diagnosed with Asperger’s. The reason I was diagnosed was working through therapy for marriage difficulties. It turns out that I mistake many of my wife’s emotions as attacks again me. After these mistakes she gets mad at me in earnest. My wife suffered abuse as a child so I took her anger as the result of that trauma. It turns out I am very deeply at fault for much of the hurt in our life. What makes the situation worse is I think my dad has Asperger’s like me (I could be wrong) and he also interprets my wife’s anger in a similarly negative light. I have been practicing a daily banishing ritual, daily devotions, and a daily cold shower since Christmas. I think these practices have allowed me to be able to see my own fault in this. I thank you JMG for those things. My question is what can I do to repair the damage and also to show others that I am really at fault as well.

Thanks
Will Oberton

replies.title: Re: Asperger’s karma
replies.content:
Okay, the first thing to do is stop blaming yourself. Aspergers syndrome is not your fault. Nor is it your fault that you got taught a dysfunctional way of relating to women while you were growing up. Yes, the combination of those two things made life really difficult for your wife, and doubtless other people as well -- and also for you! -- but you were in the position of the illiterate guy faced with a sign saying ILLITERATE? WRITE FOR FREE BOOKLET!

You and your wife have the chance now to break out of a painful and difficult rut. That's going to take quite a bit of rethinking on both your parts. You're going to have to learn how to perceive her emotions as emotions, not attacks, and develop a range of new skills for communicating. She's going to have to come to terms with the fact that you have a serious neurological impairment -- it's not just that you're not trying hard enough, it's that you can't read nonverbal cues at all. Lots of couples have come through this sort of experience with their marriage much happier and less conflicted.

One thing: let people know about the Aspergers. Do some reading about it, so you can explain it to them. It really does help when everyone knows that you're trying your best but they really do have to say things outright!

replies.title: Re: Asperger’s karma
replies.content:
Thanks John

ILLITERATE? WRITE FOR FREE BOOKLET!
That is perfect. I very much appreciate this. It has been quite the roller coaster ride figuring this all out.

comments.title: The trouble with gods
comments.content:
Something which has puzzled me for a while is the uncritical way in which many modern practitioners approach the beings they call 'Gods'. For example:
1. All gods are considered to belong to the same category. Fair enough, a student of religion may define a 'god' as a non-corporeal being who is the object of worship. But surely a modern practitioner - pagan, occultist - would apply a more discriminating approach, rather than putting all 'gods' in the same bucket.
2. Going with this, there is the often unstated assumption that all gods have our own best interests at heart. True, there is the admission the 'gods' have their own agendas and interests, but somehow, at the back of everything, one has the feeling that they are supposed to be, in the last analysis, on our side.
3. And together with this, there is the oft-repeated assertion that the 'gods' have 'agency' - but it is always assumed that the 'agency' relates to the interests and wishes of the gods themselves, and there is never any possibility that the gods may themselves be acting in the interests of other agents behind the scenes.
4. So, all in all, modern pagans and occultists are encouraged to worship and trust a set of beings about whom we know very little indeed in a real sense, in a way which might indeed be dangerous and harmful in the long run.
So my question is why is this sort of uncritical approach promoted, and why aren't the obvious pitfalls ever seriously examined?

replies.title: Re: The trouble with gods
replies.content:
I wonder if it's occurred to you that every one of these points can be applied with equal fairness to the god of the scriptural monotheist faiths:

"Something which has puzzled me for a while is the uncritical way in which many religious people approach the being they call 'God'. For example:

"1. All apparently divine manifestations are considered to come from the same god. Fair enough, a monotheist may define 'God' as the sole non-corporeal being who is the object of worship. But surely a modern worshiper would apply a more discriminating approach, rather than putting all apparent manifestations of 'God' in the same bucket.

"2. Going with this, there is the often unstated assumption that 'God' has our own best interests at heart. True, there is the admission that 'God' has his own agendas and interests, but somehow, at the back of everything, one has the feeling that he is supposed to be, in the last analysis, on our side.

"3. And together with this, there is the oft-repeated assertion that 'God' has 'agency' - but it is always assumed that the 'agency' relates to the interests and wishes of 'God' himself, and there is never any possibility that 'God' may himself be acting in the interests of other agents behind the scenes.

"4. So, all in all, modern monotheists are encouraged to worship and trust a being about whom we know very little indeed in a real sense, in a way which might indeed be dangerous and harmful in the long run.

"So my question is why is this sort of uncritical approach promoted, and why aren't the obvious pitfalls ever seriously examined?"

replies.title: Re: The trouble with gods
replies.content: Yes, of course it has.

replies.title: Re: The trouble with gods
replies.content:
Good. Most people who use that specific canned argument are evangelical Christian trolls, which is why I tend to respond in the above fashion first. Now we can try another version of the same rhetoric:

"Something which has puzzled me for a while is the uncritical way in which many modern people approach science and technology. For example:

"1. All scientific and technological discoveries are considered to belong to the same march of progress. Fair enough, a scientists may define 'Progress' as the sum total of all scientific and technological discoveries. But surely a modern person would apply a more discriminating approach, rather than putting all apparent manifestations of 'Progress' in the same bucket.

"2. Going with this, there is the often unstated assumption that 'Progress' has our own best interests at heart. True, there is the admission that 'Progress' has its own internal dynamic, but somehow, at the back of everything, one has the feeling that it is supposed to be, in the last analysis, on our side.

"3. And together with this, there is the oft-repeated assertion that 'Progress' has its own momentum - but it is always assumed that the momentum relates to the internal dynamics of 'Progress' itself, and there is never any possibility that 'Progress' may itself be acting in the interests of other agents behind the scenes.

"4. So, all in all, modern people are encouraged to worship and trust a process about whom we know very little indeed in a real sense, in a way which might indeed be dangerous and harmful in the long run.

"So my question is why is this sort of uncritical approach promoted, and why aren't the obvious pitfalls ever seriously examined?"

There are more versions, of course. It's possible to subject anything at all, including your next meal, to the same kind of rhetorical game...

replies.title: Re: The trouble with gods
replies.content: Yes, all very true, but I was asking specifically about polytheistic gods, as understood in many modern neo-pagan and occult practices, in which there would seem to be a contradiction between the professed progressiveness and open-mindedness of many of the practitioners, and their actual behaviour in reality. Surely it is wiser to take a more cautious approach, and enquire about the downsides and dangers first, before jumping in head-first. After all, one takes precautions before preparing one's next meal, if one doesn't want to get ill, and that is hardly a 'rhetorical game'.

comments.title: Apollonius of Tyana
comments.content:
Who was Apollonius of Tyana?

Was he the 'real' Jesus or just another person born during the grand conjunction that began the age of pisces, and the followers of Jesus managed to crowd out his followers?

replies.title: Re: Apollonius of Tyana
replies.content: When the Piscean era dawned, its energies inspired a new religious dispensation, and quite a few prophets and messiahs responded to that in their own ways; the followers of Jesus happened to be the most successful in one part of the world, while the followers of Muhammad, the founders of Mahayana Buddhism, and so on were more successful in other parts of the world. That's the way this sort of thing usually goes -- and of course there was a steady supply of would-be prophets and messiahs straight through the Piscean era, some of whom launched large religious movements while others didn't succeed so well. We probably have more saviors to deal with before that impulse finishes guttering out.

replies.title: Re: Apollonius of Tyana
replies.content: Apollonius of Tyana was a Neopythagorean adept and he didn't have any messianic claim. Early Church Fathers mixed his life story with the archetype of "dying-and-rising god" to create their version of Jesus myth.

I haven't seen any of your readers bring this up so far this week or the two weeks before, so I will. Three weeks ago, the New York Times published an article asking "Will Coronavirus Kill Astrology?" The premise was that, since no astrologer saw the pandemic coming, then people should lose confidence in astrology. Much to the reporter's bemusement, the premise turned out to be wrong. First, she found that people turned even more to astrologers and astrology after the pandemic began than before. Second, she did find astrologers who had seen the recession, if not the pandemic, coming. One she quoted attributed the pandemic to Pluto's influence. Of course, the reporter concluded her article with a dismissal of the whole idea that astrology actually works.
https://www.nytimes.com/2020/05/09/style/coronavirus-astrology-predictions.html

Now that I've set them up, here are my questions. First, are you the least bit surprised at any of the popular reaction, the astrologer's foreseeing trouble, if not the exact nature of it, or the reporter's attitude? Second, how does this pandemic being influence by Pluto square with your idea that Pluto is waning in influence as it moves away from the Sun after being demoted from major planet status? Finally, are viruses really part of Pluto's portfolio?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This kind of silliness shows up quite often -- usually when astrology is becoming too popular for the comfort of the chattering classes. No, I'm not surprised by any of it. As for Pluto, keep in mind that I've said repeatedly that Pluto's influence is waning -- not that it's already gone. The fact that the coronavirus outbreak has turned out to be vastly less destructive than the original models predicted suggests that it is in fact linked with Pluto, since nearly all Plutonian phenomena -- nuclear power, Freudian psychoanalysis, etc. -- showed up accompanied by loud pronouncements about how they were going to change everything, and then gradually dwindled away to end in a whimper. (So, for that matter, did Pluto; it was thought to be the mass of the Earth when originally discovered, and by the time it was downgraded it had turned out to be around 1/400 of Earth's mass.)

comments.title: Gods in Golden Dawn Practice

Hi JMG and commentariat,

I've been practising Golden Dawn style magic for around a year and a half now, and though the grade structure of the GD, its ritual forms, and the Tree of Life itself resonate quite strongly with me, I've never felt much attachment to the Hebrew God or his angels. Lately, for a variety of reasons, I've been feeling a strong pull towards Hellenism, and I have two related questions:

Am I veering too close to mixing systems here? I'm sure one could construct a purely Hellenic system à la The Celtic Golden Dawn, but I'm still very much a beginner and not (yet) in a position to do such a thing. Is this middle-ground reasonable or should I stick to the classic structure for now?

2) The importance of reading the classics goes without saying, but do you or any of my fellow commenters have reading recommendations for practising Hellenism in the modern day? I figure the quality of opinions here will take me much further than scrolling through Amazon reviews :)

Thank you for taking the time; I always learn a lot here.

replies.title: Re: Gods in Golden Dawn Practice
replies.content:
1) I'd encourage you to stick to the classic structure until you've mastered it, and then create a Hellenic Golden Dawn the way I created the Celtic Golden Dawn. I think you'll find a lot of people interested in practicing that with you.

replies.title: Re: Gods in Golden Dawn Practice

It has a well structured blend of ancient Greek spiritual exercises* adapted from different schools like Epicureans, Stoics and Neoplatonists. *These exercises are not like ceremonial magic but they can give you a solid theoretical and contemplative background useful for more complex occult practices.

comments.content:
Dear JMG,

1. Do you happen to recall whether the Watkins book discusses Elen of the Roads at all?
2. In areas outside of Britain (ie, northeastern North America), are there ways to find natural ley lines?

Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Not to the best of my recollection.

2) Okay, here my views differ from those of the pop-culture mainstream. As I understand it, there are no natural ley lines. Ley lines are artifacts; they were constructed by ancient cultures with a vastly more sophisticated knowledge of earth energies and natural magic than we have now. Where that didn't happen, there are no ley lines. That I know of, ley lines or equivalent alignments exist in western and northwestern Europe; in New England; in Japan; and in a somewhat different form, in Australia, where the "songlines" are still maintained by some Aboriginal peoples.

They may exist in some other parts of the world, but there are very large areas that seem to lack them completely. As a rule, where there are leys there are also standing stones and burial mounds -- they form part of a common toolkit of earth magic.

comments.title: Religious persecution.  Why?

replies.title: Re: Religious persecution.  Why?
replies.content: It's specifically a problem with dogmatic monotheist faiths. In Roman times, people of hundreds of religions lived in perfect amity with one another. Once people embrace the notion that there's only one god, who's given them the one true religion, and any disagreement about matters of faith and morals is thus a threat to the capital-T truth, you need fairly strong civil laws to keep them from murdering each other over religious minutiae.

Pre-publication DA student here. I'm planning on starting the Grade III Awakening of the Solar Plexus soon, but I had a few questions:

1. I assume this is fine, or even beneficial, to practice while on the Carey protocol?

2. The instructions say to return to normal breathing for "a few" minutes the first time and "several" minutes the second time. Is this the more colloquial "relax until your breathing returns to normal and then some" meaning of the terms, or should I actually get a timer and make sure multiple minutes elapse? If the latter, can I do things like brush my teeth between performances, or do I have to wait in position?

3. I can imagine quite a few different arrangements of light that fit the description of the multicolored ball of light. I was going to send you a long list of possible interpretations and ask which was correct, but then I thought maybe I was over-thinking and providing a general description meant that any specific arrangement that fit the general outline would be fine. Is this the case, or is there a specific image I was supposed to get that just went over my head? If it's general, can it be rotating?

Thank you very much!
~Turquoise Abyssopelagic Marmot

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes.

2) Don't worry about timing; just wait until your breathing settles.

3) You're overthinking it. Come up with something you like.

-- Citrine Bombastic Opossum

comments.title: A boost of WoH enthusiasm and a family question.
comments.content:
I finished WoH book one this past week and Kingsport arrived on the mail yesterday. It was so good! Working the first chapters of kingsport makes me feel like Jenny on the quad when looks for professor Akeley...

The Long Man just humbles me into how much we have lost our contact with these sort of things which seem to be just between the physical and the unseen. You have mentioned before that a lot of magic has been lost through the disenchantment of the world and the rise of industry but I haven’t really put that into perspective since Magic is a new thing in my life and there is a lot to learn, enough for me to see a lot of it which just boggles me into how much there could’ve been before... I can only wonder how many other secrets await for being rediscovered. May The Old Ones help me on that path!

The only way I knew for that phenomenon to happen was on ellipsoids forming the roof of buildings. If two people stand at each of the foci of an ellipsoid, you can see the same phenomenon as the definition of an ellipse is that it can be traced through these two points, so all the waves bounce easily between the traced surface and them. But that happening right in the open? Incredible.

——

Are these ley lines discussed the same or similar as the moon paths that feature in the WoH?

My brother grew up troubled and a lot of anger has risen in him. He walks like a volcano about to erupt and I can see on his face on pictures form this past decade just how angry he is within himself since it all started in 2012. He has been fighting with whatever is bothering him from inside for many years now and that also brings a lot of suffering for the rest of the family since interacting with him is very harsh and violent sometimes. Even though only him can work those out I think I can help to bring clarity and the right situations and support in his life for him to solve it.

I am trying with my parents to solve some of it by regularly talking about these issues and both have started to take up on prayer on their own way but they are growing older and more tired with the situation and would like to see harmony with all this issue for everyone’s good. I was thinking that a Honey Jar spell would help to lubricate a bit of the interactions between my brother and the rest of the family so that those are not factors that further worsen whatever he is dealing with and make it easier on my parents.

1) Would the honey jar work be done for each member or can it be done for the whole family? If so, how to do it?
2) Besides that I wanted to add something else that would bring peace and clarity to my brother specifically as a support as mentioned above. Would burning a candle be adequate for this situation added with planetary charity? If so what combination would work best?
3)I know that the recipe that has been given here is for a specific number of days so would it be a good idea to do that first for those days and then follow indefinitely with something like this until the situation is solved or more stable? http://www.luckymojo.com/blessing.html Would you add something else to it without resorting to ceremonial magic?
4) To which deity or planetary energy, I assume the same as the candle work, would I pray for help with this? And if so how should I phrase it? Asking for guidance, peace, courage and clarity for my brother?

Many thanks as always

replies.title: Re: A boost of WoH enthusiasm and a family question.
replies.content:
1) Thank you! I'm delighted that you're enjoying them. Yes, the moon paths in WoH are modeled on leys.

2) You can do one honey jar for the whole family -- just do it with that intention. For your brother, a light blue candle would be suitable, and you might dress it with Peaceful Home oil.

3) Yes, that would be a good approach; no, I wouldn't add anything else.

4) Venus is the planetary energy that rules over peace, happiness, love, and reconciliation. You'll have to work out the phrasing yourself; try just praying extempore, letting your feelings guide your words.

comments.content: When casting an eclipse chart, is it for the moment of the eclipse, or the moment the sun/moon reach conjunction or opposition (as the case may be)?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's a difference between modern and traditional astrology. Modern astrologers do it for the moment of maximum eclipse; traditionally, though, it was done for the moment of conjunction or opposition. I do the latter.

comments.title: French?
comments.content:
Hi,

You mentioned recently that as someone with roots in the GD that you found the French tradition a bit weird, and yet you seem familiar and comfortable enough with the material to produce a translation of Levi and enjoy Peladan. So you might be well acquainted with some of the landscape in the French lineage beyond them.

Would you be able to recommend, in the absence of local groups (e.g. Martinists) a practical form of introduction -in English - to the French tradition? I'm thinking of something (anything) comparable to your own LRM? Material available in English seem to be fairly advanced (e.g. Tomberg; the new translations of the Russian Mebes' use of the French tradition.) And Tobias Churton's 'Occult Paris' is a fun read but it's history not practice.

Is there anything for (isolated) beginners?

With thanks.

replies.title: Re: French?
replies.content: Well, how about Lévi's Doctrine and Ritual of High Magic? Might as well start from the fountainhead...
